graph.confusable_reason: Both are confused only in a narrow window: foliar discoloration and premature ripening/whitening. BYDV causes leaf yellowing/reddening with stunting in tillering-to-jointing stages, while Fusarium graminearum head blight produces bleached, prematurely-senescing spikes at heading/grain-fill that can be mistaken at a distance for the patchy chlorosis/early ripening of a BYDV-infected stand. Field scouts viewing canopy discoloration from afar, or seeing premature death of tillers, may conflate the two. Up close they diverge sharply (leaf-tip color vs. bleached spikelets with pink sporulation), so confusion is real but resolves quickly on the right plant part.
graph.shared_presentation: Patchy stand discoloration and premature, uneven ripening/senescence of wheat — affected plants stand out as off-color (yellow/tan) against a green canopy, with reduced yield and shriveled grain.

forks.question: Where does the primary, most striking symptom localize — on the leaves or on the spike/head?
forks.a_signal: Discoloration centered on leaves, especially leaf tips and margins moving inward/downward; heads if formed are small but not bleached
forks.b_signal: Discoloration centered on the spike — individual bleached/tan spikelets or whole heads while flag leaf may still be green

forks.question: At what crop stage are symptoms most evident?
forks.a_signal: Tillering to jointing; early-infected plants are severely stunted with discoloration well before heading
forks.b_signal: Anthesis (flowering) through grain fill; symptoms appear on heads after heading

forks.question: What is the color and pattern of the leaf/tissue discoloration?
forks.a_signal: Bright golden-yellow to reddish/purple discoloration starting at leaf tips, often blotchy and beginning in patches/spreading from field margins
forks.b_signal: Straw/tan bleaching of spikelets (premature whitening) rather than yellow/red leaf coloration; green leaves persist

forks.question: Is there visible fungal sign on the heads (the decisive sign-vector check)?
forks.a_signal: No fungal growth anywhere; a virus produces no signs — only host discoloration and stunting; aphids may be present as vector
forks.b_signal: Salmon-pink to orange sporodochia in the spikelet bracts/rachis (and blue-black perithecia later); pink-tinged shriveled 'tombstone' kernels

forks.question: How does the discoloration develop and distribute over time?
forks.a_signal: Stunting plus discoloration that is persistent and tied to early infection; whole plant dwarfed, reddening intensifies with cool weather
forks.b_signal: Bleaching spreads up or down from the point of infection within a head; partial heads (some spikelets dead, others green) are classic

forks.question: What weather/conditions preceded symptom onset?
forks.a_signal: Mild fall/spring favoring aphid flights and feeding; symptoms after aphid colonization, worse in cool sunny weather (color intensifies)
forks.b_signal: Warm, wet/humid conditions during flowering (anthesis); rain-splash and prolonged wetness at heading drive infection

graph.narrative_a: Barley Yellow Dwarf Virus is an aphid-vectored luteovirus that infects phloem and disrupts carbohydrate movement, producing the classic triad of stunting, leaf discoloration, and reduced tillering/heading. Discoloration begins at leaf tips and margins, golden-yellow in wheat and often with reddish-purple tints, progressing inward and downward; early-infected plants are dramatically dwarfed. Symptoms emerge from tillering through jointing and frequently appear in patches or spreading from field edges following aphid arrival. Critically, a virus produces no fungal signs — there is no mold, no pink sporulation, no perithecia — only host responses. Aphids (bird cherry-oat, English grain, greenbug) may be found and serve as the vector clue.
graph.narrative_b: Fusarium graminearum on wheat most conspicuously causes Fusarium Head Blight (scab): after infection during warm, humid flowering, individual spikelets or whole heads bleach to a straw/tan color while the rest of the plant and even the flag leaf can remain green. The diagnostic sign is salmon-pink to orange sporodochia in the bracts and at the base of spikelets along the rachis, with blue-black perithecia developing later; infected kernels become shriveled, chalky-white to pink 'tombstones' and may carry deoxynivalenol (DON) mycotoxin. The same fungus also causes seedling blight and crown/root rot, but the head phase is what overlaps superficially with BYDV's premature-ripening appearance.
graph.management_a: Manage BYDV through cultural and vector tactics: delay fall planting to avoid peak aphid flights, plant tolerant/resistant varieties (Bdv2 and other tolerance genes), control volunteer cereals and grassy 'green bridge' hosts, and use insecticidal seed treatments (neonicotinoid) or scout-and-spray foliar insecticides targeting aphids before they colonize. No curative options exist once plants are infected; prevention of aphid feeding is the lever.
graph.management_b: Manage Fusarium head blight with an integrated program: moderately resistant varieties, avoid planting wheat into corn or wheat residue (rotation/tillage to reduce inoculum), and apply a triazole fungicide (e.g., prothioconazole + tebuconazole, metconazole) at early anthesis (Feekes 10.5.1) timed to predicted wet flowering weather using FHB risk forecasting tools. Harvest promptly, adjust combine to blow out lightweight scabby kernels, and test grain for DON.

lay.farmer_lay_report: I've got patches in the field where the wheat is turning yellow and tan and ripening way too early, while the rest of the field is still green. Those off-color plants look a little stunted and stand out, and the grain heads there are filling poorly with shriveled, lightweight kernels.
lay.decisive_lay_a: No fuzz or growth of any kind on the plants at all, just the yellowing and stunting itself, and you may spot small soft-bodied bugs (aphids) on the leaves that brought the trouble in.
lay.decisive_lay_b: Salmon-pink to orange coloring on the heads, right in the little chaffy parts of the grain head, sometimes with tiny blue-black specks later, plus pinkish, shriveled, chalky "tombstone" kernels.
